course_description:
Are burrowing cockroaches the best parents? Are dragonflies the ultimate predator? Are scale insects the ultimate sex machine? This course brings together basic anatomy and physiology and relates this to behaviour. The main physiological systems will be examined sequentially and aspects of biology relevant to currently active or promising research areas featured. The course features aspects of physiology and anatomy from pest insects to beneficial biocontrol agents, from developmental model organisms to ones that have only been recently discovered. Strap in for an entomological adventure!

BIOL3238 may be completed for students declaring a Minor in Entomology, but would also be suitable for anyone with a broad and keen interest in entomology. This is not an insect identification course (see BIOL2205 or BIOL3232 instead), but rather this is a course focussed on contemporary techniques and theories used in applied entomology . ﾠ

prerequisites: You'll need to complete the following courses before enrolling in this one:
companions: We recommend completing the following courses at the same time:

timetable:
The timetable for this course is available on the
UQ Public Timetable
.
Additional timetable information
Classes do not occur every week. Check the weekly timetable for specific instructions. At the mid-point of the semester, you will dedicate a three week block on a research project where the lab will operate as an 'open lab'. You should manage your own time during this window for the completion of your project. Further information will be provided to you in class.
course_aims: This aims of this course are toﾠprovide:

learning_outcomes.description: Understand insect physiology in varied environmental, economic and social contexts
Relate form with function to explain physiological adaptations in insects
Describe how insect physiology is experimentally studied and manipulated in various applied entomology contexts
Read and appraise entomological literature critically
Demonstrate technical and professional engagement skills related to future careers in entomology

assessment_details.title: Professional Engagement
assessment_details.other_conditions: Student specific, Longitudinal, Secure. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: Throughout the semester, you will be engaging with lecturers in simulated work environments related to entomological research. This includes: Applied learning (APP) classes where you are working systematically through varied activities including but not limited to journal clubs, computer-based problem-solving, discussions etc. Researchers engage frequently in similar settings to share their findings, discuss recent work done by other scientists, and strategize the direction of their own research. Practicals (PRA) where you will conduct your own mini-research project, including ideation, experimental design, execution, data collection and analysis of results. You will not be required to produce a formal scientific report per se but you are expected to maintain a detailed logbook and journal that is hand-written (with the exception of statistical analyses which should be provided in graphical form and where relevant accompanying code). Based on your engagement with your lecturers throughout the semester, and any evidence submitted by the end of the research project, your holistic professional engagement will be assessed. The key areas of your professionalism we are interested in: proficiency in professional communication (in written form but also including non-verbal cues) proficiency in professional behaviour (including risk management and lab safety) proficiency in scientific research skills proficiency in data analysis skills (bonus) your leadership skills (we will discuss this in class) Evidence to assess may includes: your active participation during class, during group conversations or one-on-one engagement with your lecturers your participation on Discussion Board interaction (collegiality) with your peers your time management and commitment (including punctuality) the quality of your proposed and executed experiment the quality of your note-taking in your research logbook the quality of your data analysis and data visualisation (from your project data) your ability to solve problems during unexpected events while you undertake your research project This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Online submission by Turnitin only by the due date and time. Refer to Blackboard for the submission link. No hard copy or assignment cover sheets are required. Submission via email is not accepted.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
You are required to submit assessable items on time. If you fail to meet the submission deadline for any assessment item, then 10% of the maximum possible mark for the assessment item (assessment ‘marked from’ value) will be deducted as a late penalty for every day (or part day) late after the due date. For example, if you submit your assignment 1 hour late, you will be penalised 10%; if your assignment is 24.5 hours late, you will be penalised 20% (because it is late by one 24-hour period plus part of another 24-hour period).

assessment_details.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: Hot Topics Presentation
assessment_details.other_conditions: Student specific, Time limited, Secure. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: You will deliver an engaging 5 minute presentation based on your own desktop research about an area related to applied entomology. The topic of your presentation will be randomly assigned to you in Week 5 of semester. You should deliver the presentation verbally, and may use visual aids (slides) or props. You are being assessed on: ability to distil credible evidence (primary literature) to adequately address your assigned topic ability to communicate genuinely, authentically and confidently ability to think critical and provoke curiosity through constructive criticism ability to respond to questions effectively (during a dedicated Q&A session after your presentation) Some example topics: Why haven't we discovered all the stick insects? Are sex pheromone traps really effective? Are ants in the movie ANTZ portrayed accurately? Are biological control strategies a waste of time and money? Will fire ants ever be eradicated in Queensland? This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
You will be scheduled to deliver 5 minutes presentation in-person, and submit a copy via Turnitin link by the due date and time. Refer to Blackboard for the submission link. No hard copy or assignment cover sheets are required. Submission via email is not accepted.
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
This assessment may only be eligible for extension if exceptional circumstances arise on the day of the scheduled presentation. Please contact your course coordinator with supporting documentation of the exceptional circumstances that prevented you from attending for consideration of an alternate arrangement.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
You are required to submit assessable items on time. If you fail to meet the submission deadline for any assessment item, then 10% of the maximum possible mark for the assessment item (the assessment ‘marked from’ value) will be deducted as a late penalty for every day (or part day) late after the due date.
For example, if you submit your assignment 1 hour late, you will be penalised 10%; if your assignment is 24.5 hours late, you will be penalised 20% (because it is late by one 24-hour period plus part of another 24-hour period).

assessment_details.title: Collaborative Examination
assessment_details.other_conditions: Time limited, Secure. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: You will complete the Collaborative Exam during a scheduled time during the end-of-semester exam block. More information will be provided in class. The exam will assess content on: self-directed learning (SDL) modules applied learning (APP) classes that have taken place throughout the semester During the exam, you may: refer to any resources and have internet access (essentially, it is an open-book test) engage in discussion with your peers You should expect questions in the exam to assess your higher-order thinking skills, critical reading skills, and written communication skills. There will be little to no questions in the exam that requires you to rely on recalling knowledge. This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.exam_details: Planning time no planning time minutes Duration 120 minutes Calculator options No calculators permitted Open/closed book Open book examination - any written or printed material is permitted; material may be annotated Exam platform Paper based Invigilation Invigilated in person
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Deferral or extension
You may be able to defer this exam .
While you may be able to defer this exam, please note that you will not have the option to collaborate with your peers during the deferred exam.

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life. As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments for Students Policy and Procedure AI for Assessment Guide Recording of Teaching Policy and Procedure Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library .
